messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Articulate the novel and ambitious elements. Explain what makes this genuinely breakthrough and beyond state of the art.
Write the Ambition and innovation beyond the state of the art section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.4 OTHER KEY ASPECTS OF OUR PROPOSED WORK  
In addition to our prMRF development discussed above, a number of complementary activities are envisaged which address the requirements of the HORIZON-CL4-2021-DIGITAL-EMERGING-01-09 call, as summarized below.  
Training Programs. We will organize training programs focused on state-of-the-art waste management technologies (WP1, Task1.5). Besides being open to the public, these programs will be mainly designed for partners ION, HRRC, AIMPLAS, AXIA, ISWA that are staffed with waste management experts who need to know more about the differences of RGB and hyperspectral imaging, the power and the limitations of AI and Deep Learning, the available mechanical/robotic systems involved in the automated treatment of waste, etc.  
Open Science Practices. We fully support the Open Science practices in the sharing of knowledge, data and tools as early as possible in our project’s lifetime. To this end, we include the implementation of the following actions:  
o Use of open research infrastructures for knowledge and data sharing, such as the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) that provides seamless access, FAIR (Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability and Reusability) management, and reliable reuse of research data and all other digital objects produced along the research life cycle (e.g. methods, software and publications).  
o Early and open sharing of research data and results, through preprints, or informal publications on our project’s website and public repositories.  
o Direct and open sharing of research data collected by citizens with the help of recycling data-games through public repositories.  
o Open access to research results and reports, either by publishing them with full open access in established, highly popular peer-reviewed scientific publications, or by rapidly sharing them at no cost, through focused publications in the Open Research Europe Publishing Platform (OREPP).  
o Data Management Planning following the “as open as possible as closed as necessary” principle, which will ensure data and results are deposited in trusted repositories, they are physically or digitally accessible, they are licensed for public free use (e.g. under “CC BY”), with potential exceptions clearly specified.  
Further to these general directions, we already include concrete actions that promote Open Science. In particular, to increase the quality, efficiency and impact of research and innovation activities, and accelerate responsiveness to societal challenges related to recycling, the RGB and hyperspectral waste imaging data will be made publicly available to create multiplicative effects in EU research focused on digital waste processing and particularly the automated identification and categorization of recyclables. The data will be supplemented with benchmarks so that we can compare the performance of our newly developed algorithms. The relevant repositories will be elaborated in the Data Management Plan deliverable, D1.1. Moreover, to boost societal engagement in research and innovation and increase the trust of society in science, the citizen-annotated data via recycling data games will be made directly and openly available to their creators and the wider public for exploitation and re-use. The above actions are in full agreement with the Horizon Europe Key Impact Pathways that address open science practices, namely the ‘Fostering diffusion of knowledge and open science’ pathway focused on scientific impact, and the ‘Strengthening the uptake of R&I in society’ pathway focused on societal impact.  
Management of data and other research outputs. The preparation of a Data Management Plan (DMP) will ensure that our research datasets will be managed in line with the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). The DMP will follow the “as open as possible as closed as necessary” principle, which will ensure data and results are deposited in trusted repositories, are digitally (if necessary physically) accessible, and are licensed for public free use (e.g. under “CC BY”) with potential exceptions clearly specified.  
Types of data/research output: we will collect, manage, and generate the following types of datasets: (i) waste imaging data with the use of RGB/hyperspectral cameras and annotations on these data gathered via recycling data games, (ii) the recycling data games and statistics on the use of these games, (iii) AI/Deep Learning models accomplishing the identification, localization and categorization of targeted recyclable materials appearing on these images, (iv) outputs regarding the performance of these models to be used as benchmarks for future research, (v) operational scenarios and performance statistics of our RoReWo Team and the complete equipment of the material recovery chain installed in the prMRF, (vi) videos of training programs on the use of digital and smart technologies for material recovery and citizen engagement.  
HORIZON-CL4-2021-DIGITAL-EMERGING-01-09: AI, data and Robotics for the Green Deal (IA) [RECLAIM]                                 Page 17 of 45  
Findability: the above datasets will be published as open data in relevant open repositories where a dedicated project space will be established, e.g. European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) and OpenAIRE’s Zenodo. The datasets will be published following the FAIR Digital Object Framework. We will use globally resolvable persistent identifiers that enable metadata indexing, such as Handles/DOIs for the FAIR indexing of data objects and the specification of data access and management policies associated with them.  
Accessibility: The datasets will be made available on well-recognized repositories (e.g. EOSC, OpenAIRE’s Zenodo) that ensure reliable access to research data relevant digital objects. Further to the above, recycling data-games will be freely accessible and usable through focused gaming networks and repositories (e.g. OS Game Clones, GitHub). Other physical project developments will be easily and freely accessible by interested stakeholders and the wider public.  
Interoperability: To ensure the interoperability of published datasets, we will design structure and publish the data using digital data models based on standards and ontologies (e.g. Industrial Ontologies Foundry). We will also consider the introduction of new data types, in communication with Data Type Registries (e.g. Cordra, ePIC).  
Reusability: The datasets will be made available for public access and reuse under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International Public License (CC BY 4.0) or a license with similar access permissions.  
Curation and storage/preservation costs: We aim to utilize zero-cost data repositories (EOSC, OpenAIRE’s Zenodo). Moreover, we have access to infrastructures that allow the hosting of data and the immediate, fast access to it. In any case, we will bear all ongoing costs to dataset curation operations.  
Dissemination/Communication. We will actively disseminate the expected/accomplished project results to the relevant stakeholders and target audience (see section 2.2.1). Additionally, with the support of all partners, ISWA will exploit the already established global communication channels to highlight, from project day-one, the new opportunities provided by our prMRF for highly effective, distributed material recovery.  
Relation to the `do no significant harm' principle (Article 17 of Regulation (EU) No 2020/852). Our proposed work provides a clear and direct support to the fourth and fifth objectives of the EU Taxonomy Regulation, in particularly the "Transition to a circular economy" and the "Pollution prevention and control". Moreover, it provides an indirect support to the other four objectives of the same regulation without being against or harming any of the objectives by any means. Accordingly, we foster environmentally sustainable activities that contribute to the EU’s environmental objectives, promote sustainable investments and ultimately support the implementation of the European Green Deal.  
Gender Issues. Our project planning and activities are compliant with Council Resolution 1999/C 201/01 which states that “the gender mainstreaming of research policy is not limited to promotion of women as research workers but should also ensure that research meets the needs of all citizens and when necessary contributes to the understanding of gender-relevant issues”. The main research stream of our project focuses on material recovery with the minimum possible human intervention and therefore, it does not address research or technology topics related to human gender. In particular, upon successful completion of our project, all people regardless of gender, will be equally treated by the developed prMRF. On the other side, the recycling data-game work plan assumes active participation of users for testing and refinement. Besides the fact that the envisioned games do not assume any kind of functional differentiation between the sexes, we will carefully and equally promote the game into men and women, considering the recruitment of a gender balanced mix of users, to provide unbiased guidance for planned developments.  
Moreover, we will balance the gender distribution of the research and technical staff involved in our project by following non-discriminating hiring practices and trying to address the EC goal of 40% participation of women at all levels, in implementing and managing EU-funded programmes.  
Multidisciplinarity. We tie together complementary technologies to promote and support the goals of the EU Green Deal. To accomplish our objectives, we adopt a multidisciplinary approach combining expertise from different fields such as Data science, AI, robotics, mechanical engineering, optics, scientific gaming, environmental science, industrial management, innovation management, governmental authorities, citizen science to develop novel still feasible and directly applicable solutions on material recovery and to increase social awareness on recycling and the circular economy. Our interdisciplinary approach is summarized in Table 1.3.  
HORIZON-CL4-2021-DIGITAL-EMERGING-01-09: AI, data and Robotics for the Green Deal (IA) [RECLAIM]                                 Page 18 of 45  
Table 1.3 Overview of the multidisciplinary approach adopted in RECLAIM

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Articulate the novel and ambitious elements. Explain what makes this genuinely breakthrough and beyond state of the art.
Write the Ambition and innovation beyond the state of the art section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.4 Ambition  
To support the European Strategy for Plastics in a Circular Economy and contribute to alleviating the environmental problems faced by plastic waste contamination, we have the ambition to provide biotechnology-based tools (involving microorganisms, novel enzymes, earthworms, and insects) with plastic degrading capabilities that will be suitable for application in ex-situ (composting, vermicomposting, reactor, insect chambers) or in-situ (soil) treatment of plastic contaminated matrix. Biotech solutions will include symbiotic bioprocesses between previously reported high-powered microorganisms through microbiotic systems vectored by several insect species. In addition, the outputs of these processes will be chitin from insects and biofertilizers from insects, earthworms, larvae, etc. Our ground-breaking project objectives will lead to significant knowledge advancements to the state-of-the-art (SoA):  
A. State of the art and advances in the field of plastic controlled waste management and treatment technologies  
Plastic waste fractions pose a serious environmental threat due to their non-biodegradable and recalcitrant nature. Besides, physical degradation of plastics in the environment produces secondary microplastics that cause even higher negative impact. Agroecosystems are likely to be the most plastic-contaminated terrestrial system outside of landfills and urban spaces. The source of these microplastics is recycled wastewater, biosolids, and compost derived from non-source-separated residential waste or mixed municipal solid waste. Microplastics do not disappear naturally and, when mixed with soil, lead to a decline in soil quality by hindering the transfer of water and fertilizer between soil and plants. The final processing step for compost is typically sieving using 8-, 10-, or even 15-mm mesh sizes. Microplastics, defined as particles smaller than 5 mm, will therefore pass through these sieves and enter the compost. The quality and quantity of plastics to be recycled is a major obstacle to the development of this recycling process. The fact that the microplastics present in municipal waste consist of mixtures of different types of plastics, each consisting of a mixture of synthetic polymers, residual monomers, and chemical additives adds a little more complexity to the problem. The recycling of multilayer films, used broadly both in packaging and agriculture sectors, is still an important problem, which currently is not carried out on an industrial scale. There are several reasons for this, such as the large variety of materials used for each layer, large differences in the processing properties of the materials used for multilayer films, lack of systems for identification of multilayer film (e.g., PA/PE, PS/PE, PVC/PE, PET/PE, etc.), lack of system solutions for the collection of these materials, which results in storage with other foil materials with similar visible characteristics (colour, thickness, stiffness), lack of standard research of the properties, processing, and applications of composites based on recycled multicomponent materials, and lack of economically viable systems of segregation of the various materials. To some extent, flexible packaging suffers similar difficulties in their recycling even when monolayer due to their difficult sorting and handling (separators are more suitable for rigid counterparts). The collection, transport, and treatment of these mixed plastic waste is difficult and often they end up in landfills, or in the best option to incineration. In RECOVER, with the help of plastic monitoring tools, equipment, and logistic studies as stated in SoA below (C), we will propose a strategy for the targeted plastic waste collection and delivery to the appropriate degradation plant and in cases where a collection is not seen viable and an in situ (soil, compost) degradation is preferable, this will be highlighted (WP1).  
B. State of the art and advances in the field of Biotech tools for plastic biodegradation  
The biodegradation of plastics in the environment is a complex process that involves a combination of many abiotic and biotic factors. After fragmentation or modification accomplished by abiotic factors and microorganisms, the bulk polymer becomes more available for biological attack. Extracellular depolymerizing enzymes from microorganisms release monomers that are further intracellularly metabolized by them. Consequently, microorganisms and their enzymes are ultimately responsible for the complete mineralization, which is the transformation to carbon dioxide (CO2), and the final removal. Research on the biodegradation of recalcitrant synthetic polymers with carbon-carbon backbones, namely PS, PVC, PP, and PEs, has proved that they can be attacked, under different lab and actual site conditions, by a variety of mixed microbial communities and enzymes and pure fungal or bacterial strains obtained from terrestrial or aquatic environments and from some earthworm and insect larval gut. Earthworms and insect larvae are also able to degrade plastics in which process their gut microbiota play a key role. Most work in this area has been focused on PUR and PET that are easier to biodegrade but biodegradation of other more recalcitrant plastics or mixtures are also scarcely covered. Beyond the SoA, we will develop biotechnology-based solutions to mitigate the challenges associated with AWP management by providing biotech-tools (in WP2) that are able to biodegrade AWP, namely:  
- Microbial consortia and novel enzymes with faster AWP biodegradation ability;  
- Earthworms with improved capabilities for AWP biodegradation;  
- Insect larvae with improved capabilities for AWP biodegradation.  
Such synergistic combinations were never reported before in the literature. Bacteria reported for their prospective use in biodegradation of plastics include species of the genera Pseudomonas, Streptomyces, Rhodococcus, Comamonas, Clostridium, and Butyrivibrio. Among the bacteria, one that stands out as a potential candidate for plastic biodegradation is Pseudomonas because of its diverse metabolic and stress-resistance capabilities and genetic plasticity. Several fungi also have the potential to degrade plastics in aquatic and soil environments. Aspergillus, Fusarium, Pycnoporus, and Mucor are among those proposed for the biodegradation of plastics. In addition, fungi capable of degrading lignin in nature are of particular interest with respect to particularly inert structural elements of plastics such as C–C or C–Cl bonds. Lignin resembles certain plastics in not being hydrolysable and possessing similar structural elements that are oxidized and cleaved during lignin degradation by enzymes like laccase and lignin peroxidases that might enable them to degrade the C–C bond-based PE and PP. To date, the rates achieved by pure microbial cultures have been low and almost all isolates are restricted to growth on a single type of plastic under optimal lab scale conditions. Consequently, we need microorganisms and enzymes that can more rapidly and efficiently degrade complex plastic mixtures.  
Microbial consortia can improve degradation capabilities and promote the degradation of a more diverse group of plastics. For example, the decomposition of micro-sized polyethylene (PE) by mesophilic mixed bacterial culture obtained from a municipal landfill sediment reduced the dry weight of particles (18% after 60 d). Also, microbial communities isolated from plastic garbage processing areas have been used for enhanced degradation of low-density polyethylene (LDPE) strips and pellets with weight reductions of 81% and 38%, respectively, after 120 days. The enzymes produced by microorganisms offer an additional alternative for plastic biodegradation. The enzymes reported to be involved in the breakage of polymers are cutinases, esterases, laccases, lipases, and enzymes involved in lignin metabolism. These are moderately active on hydrolysable polymers such as PET and PUR but no enzymes acting on the high molecular weight polymers such as PS and PVC are known. There are a few examples of bacterial and fungal laccases and peroxidases that reduce the molecular mass of PE and cutinases and lipases with PET-degrading capacity. Using a combination of enzymes is also known to enhance polymer hydrolysis. Hydrolytic enzymes are already produced in bulk quantities and low cost for a variety of applications. The French firm Carbios (to which Carbiolice, who in our AB and committed to collaborate with RECOVER, is affiliated) has optimized an enzyme that depolymerizes 97% of pretreated PET into monomers in 24 hours. The enzyme Carbios patent mentions bacterial and fungal cutinase and lipase enzymes. Hence, the design of novel enzymatic degradation modules employing plastic hydrolases with high rates of plastic depolymerization is possible. Beyond this, considering that the diversity of known enzymes and microbes acting on plastic polymers is still rather limited, their degrading activity is low and the action on complex plastic mixtures is not currently covered, we aim to explore the diversity of the cultivated and non-cultivated microorganisms (metagenomes) to seek the building up of microbial communities and the identification of novel enzyme biocatalysts for AWP biodegradation. The search and final design of mixed tailored microbial/enzyme will be adjusted to each AWP degradation scenario, i.e., soil, compost, reactor, or feeding earthworm & insect larvae. As for the design of novel enzymes, knowledge-based design of enzymes is very poorly established, and the underlying understanding of structure-function relationships is so partial as to limit it substantially. Specific exceptions exist, such as strategies for P450 enzymes through biophysical property modification of active sites (the basis of Oxford Biotrans technology) or modular construction. Anyway, these are rare and based upon modification of unusually well-characterized enzymes. Frequently the largest influencers of enzyme activity do not lie within the active sites, and the most impactful random mutagenesis-generated improvements often lie in locations that could not have been predicted; and often are not understood even after they are identified. As a result, the principal source of new enzymes remains biological prospecting and screening for new activities and substrates. We have identified metabolic enzymes
